Checking your Nushell config for a `c` alias or command.
Checking the Nushell config: `c` is defined as an alias for `clear`.
Checking for config load errors and Nushell's current alias syntax:

input.explanation: Check if Nushell removed or changed the alias keyword in recent versions.

content.text: Checking whether the `alias` keyword was removed or changed in Nushell 0.111:

input.search_term: nushell 0.111 alias keyword removed breaking change
input.explanation: Check if alias was removed in recent Nushell (user has 0.111).

input.description: Test if alias works in current Nushell

content.text: Confirming the alias works in a minimal test. Checking whether your full config loads:

input.description: Test if dotfiles config loads and alias c exists

content.text: Checking how the mise and starship modules are loaded:

Identifying the cause: the config fails to load because of a missing module:

```
use ~/.cache/mise/init.nu
```
‚Üí `~/.cache/mise/init.nu` not found. In Nushell, `use` is evaluated at parse time, so the whole config fails and aliases (including `c = clear`) never get registered. Making the mise (and starship) init optional so the config loads when those files are missing:
Checking: `use` is parse-time in Nushell ‚Äî the parser resolves modules before running the script, so wrapping it in `if` won't help. Checking whether we can use `source` for these init scripts:

input.search_term: nushell use vs source module parse time
input.explanation: Confirm whether use is parse-time and if source can replace it for init scripts.

content.text: Reverting the change and commenting out the problematic `use` so the config can load. `use` (and paths for `source`) are resolved at parse time, so a runtime `if` won't prevent the missing-file error.

input.new_string:
source ~/.zoxide.nu
source ~/.cache/carapace/init.nu
source ~/.local/share/atuin/init.nu
use ~/.cache/starship/init.nu
# Uncomment after generating: mise activate nushell (creates ~/.cache/mise/init.nu)
# use ~/.cache/mise/init.nu

content.text: Verifying the config loads. Checking whether the starship init file exists:

input.description: Check if starship and mise init.nu exist
Verify config loads and alias c is defined

content.text: Commenting out the other missing file so the config can load.

input.new_string:
$env.DIRENV_LOG_FORMAT = ""

# Uncomment if you have wt.nu (e.g. from wezterm/wt integration)
# source ~/.config/nushell/vendor/autoload/wt.nu

input.command: nu -c 'source /Users/axos-agallentes/.dotfiles/nushell/config.nu; scope aliases | where name == c' 2>&1
input.description: Confirm config loads and alias c is registered

content.text:
Summary of what was going on and what was changed:

**Cause:** Nushell wasn‚Äôt loading your config because of **missing files** that are loaded at parse time:

1. **`~/.cache/mise/init.nu`** ‚Äì missing (never created, e.g. by `mise activate nushell`).
2. **`~/.config/nushell/vendor/autoload/wt.nu`** ‚Äì missing.

When Nushell hits a missing file for `use` or `source`, the whole config fails to load, so **none of your config (including `alias c = clear`) was applied**. That‚Äôs why `c` was ‚Äúnot found‚Äù and Nushell suggested `cd`.

**Changes made:**

1. **`use ~/.cache/mise/init.nu`** ‚Äì Commented out and a short note added. After you run `mise activate nushell` (or whatever creates that file), you can uncomment it.
2. **`source ~/.config/nushell/vendor/autoload/wt.nu`** ‚Äì Commented out with a note. Uncomment it if you add that wt.nu file (e.g. for WezTerm).

After restarting Nushell (or opening a new session), your config should load and **`c`** should work as **`clear`**.

**Optional ‚Äì get mise init back:**  
If you use mise and want its Nushell integration:

```bash
mise activate nushell
```

That should create `~/.cache/mise/init.nu`. Then in `config.nu` you can uncomment:

```nu
# use ~/.cache/mise/init.nu
```

Checking `env.nu` for the mise setup:
Making the mise setup in `env.nu` conditional so we don't run it when `mise` isn't available:

Updated `env.nu` so the mise setup no longer breaks startup when `mise` isn‚Äôt installed or not in PATH.

**Change:** The `^mise activate nu | save -f ~/.cache/mise/init.nu` call is now inside a `try { ... } catch { ... }`. If `mise` isn‚Äôt found, the error is caught and startup continues.

Start Nushell again with `nu`. Once `mise` is installed and on your PATH, that block will run and create `~/.cache/mise/init.nu` automatically. You can also run `mise activate nushell` yourself to generate it.

<user_query>
# Smart Commit and Push

Analyze the current git changes and intelligently split them into **atomic commits**, then push them to the remote repository.

## Instructions

1. Run `git status` and `git diff` to see all current changes
2. Analyze the changes and identify candidate groups based on:
   - Files in the same directory or subdirectory
   - Related functionality (e.g., all documentation changes, all configuration changes)
   - Dependency relationships (e.g., tests that go with implementation files)
   - Similar types of changes (additions, modifications, deletions)

3. Ensure groups are **atomic**:
   - Each commit must represent **one logical change** (smallest sensible unit)
   - If a group contains multiple independent changes, split it further until each commit is atomic
   - Avoid mixing refactors with behavior changes unless inseparable

4. For each atomic group:
   - Stage the relevant files using `git add`
   - Create a descriptive commit message that:
     - Follows **Conventional Commits**: https://www.conventionalcommits.org/en/v1.0.0/
       - Format: `type(scope): summary`
       - Common `type`: `feat`, `fix`, `docs`, `refactor`, `test`, `chore`, `ci`, `build`, `perf`, `style`, `revert`
       - Use **!** or **BREAKING CHANGE:** (or both) in the body when applicable
     - Follows the repository's commit message style (check recent commits with `git log`)
     - Focuses on the "why" rather than just the "what"
   - Include an **intelligent footer** (trailers at the end of the commit message):
     - If running in **Cursor**, include:
       ```
       ü§ñ Generated with Cursor
       ```
     - If running in **Claude Code**, include:
       ```
       ü§ñ Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

       Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
       ```
     - If unclear which tool is being used, **omit the footer** rather than guessing.
   - Commit the changes using a HEREDOC format

5. After all commits are created:
   - Run `git status` to verify all changes have been committed
   - Push to the remote repository using `git push`
   - Display the results to the user

## Important Notes

- DO NOT commit files that likely contain secrets (.env, credentials.json, etc.)
- If unclear about how to group changes, ask the user for guidance
- Show the user your grouping plan before making commits
- Use HEREDOC format for commit messages to ensure proper formatting
- Follow safe git practices (never force push to main/master, never skip hooks, etc.)
</user_query>

content.text:
Summary:

---

## Smart commit and push

### Grouping

| # | Scope | Files | Rationale |
|---|--------|--------|-----------|
| 1 | **fix(nushell)** | `nushell/config.nu`, `nushell/env.nu` | Single fix: config and env load when optional modules (mise, wt.nu) are missing. |
| 2 | **chore(nvim)** | `nvim/lazy-lock.json` | Lockfile-only update after plugin sync. |

**Not committed:** `.cursor/` (agent-tools, agent-transcripts) left untracked as local IDE state.

---

### Commits created

1. **`ea0dd25`** ‚Äî `fix(nushell): make config load when optional modules are missing`  
   - Commented out optional `use ~/.cache/mise/init.nu` and `source ~/.config/nushell/vendor/autoload/wt.nu`.  
   - Wrapped `mise activate` in try/catch in `env.nu`.

2. **`deb9d15`** ‚Äî `chore(nvim): update lazy-lock.json`  
   - Updated plugin pins and lockfile (adds helm-ls.nvim, updates many plugins).

---

### Push

```
To https://github.com/arjaygg/dotfiles-omerxx.git
   83ec59b..deb9d15  main -> main
```

All tracked changes are committed and pushed. The only remaining untracked items are under `.cursor/`; say if you want those added or ignored via `.gitignore`.
